history: From Arabic amir al-bahr, 'commander of the sea.' The title came to Europe through contact with Arab naval forces in the Mediterranean. Medieval Europeans, confused by the Arabic title structure, sometimes shortened it to 'amiral' and sometimes mistook al- as part of the word. The -d- in English admiral appears to be a misreading of the Arabic.

From Arabic al-kohl, originally referring to finely powdered antimony used as an eye cosmetic (kohl). Medieval alchemists extended the term to any fine powder, then to purified essences and spirits obtained by distillation. By the 17th century it specifically meant the intoxicating element in fermented drinks.

From Arabic al-jabr, part of the title of a 9th-century treatise by mathematician al-Khwarizmi: 'Al-kitab al-mukhtasar fi hisab al-jabr wal-muqabala.' Al-jabr referred to the operation of moving terms from one side of an equation to the other — 'reuniting broken parts.' Medieval scholars borrowed the title word directly.

From medieval Latin assassinus, from Arabic Hashshashin — a nickname for a secretive Nizari Ismaili sect encountered by Crusaders. The group was said to use hashish before carrying out targeted killings, though historians debate whether this was true or slander by enemies. The name stuck as a general term for hired killers.

From Arabic awariya, meaning goods damaged by seawater during shipping. When cargo was damaged, merchants who shared the ship divided the financial loss proportionally among themselves — they calculated the 'average' cost. The mathematical sense of a middle value developed from this mercantile practice.

From Arabic qahwa, a term for wine that was extended to the dark stimulating drink brewed from roasted beans. Coffee originated in Ethiopia, spread to Yemen where Sufi monks used it to stay awake during prayers, then spread through the Arab world. The Italians adapted it to caffè, giving English 'café' and 'caffeine.'

From Arabic qutn, the word for the cotton plant and its fibers. Arab traders controlled the cotton trade across North Africa, the Middle East, and into Europe during the medieval period. The word traveled with the trade routes through Italian cotone and French coton before entering English.

From Old Spanish azar, from Arabic az-zahr (the dice). It entered English through a dice game called hazard that Crusaders learned from Arab traders in the Middle East. By the 16th century, the gambling connotation expanded to mean any chance or risk.

Derived from hazard (Arabic az-zahr, the dice), with the addition of the English suffix -ous. Hazard entered English from the dice game in the 13th century; hazardous followed as a natural derivative once the base word meant 'risk or danger.' The dice-game origin shows how gambling vocabulary often migrates into everyday risk language.

From Arabic makhazin, plural of makhzan (storehouse, treasury). The word entered English via French and Italian to mean a storage room for arms and ammunition (gunpowder 'magazines' in forts). Printed publications adopted the name metaphorically in the 17th century, positioning themselves as storehouses of information and entertainment.

From Arabic matrah, 'a place where something is thrown or laid down,' from taraha (to throw). Arab bedding — cushions or padded mats laid on the floor — was far more comfortable than European straw bedding, and Crusaders returning from the Holy Land brought both the product and its name back to Europe.

From Arabic ta'rifa, 'notification' or 'inventory of fees,' from arrafa (to notify). The word entered European languages through trade with Arab merchants in the Mediterranean. A tariff was originally a list posted at a port or market showing the fees due on various goods — an official notification of charges.

From Arabic sifr (empty), a translation of Sanskrit shunya (void). Arab mathematicians transmitted the Indian concept of zero — a placeholder representing nothing — to medieval Europe. The concept was revolutionary: without zero, arithmetic is crippled. Medieval Europeans initially resisted zero as a suspicious foreign idea.

From French ballet, from Italian balletto (little dance), diminutive of ballo (ball, dance). Ballet as an art form was developed in the Italian Renaissance courts of the 15th–16th centuries, then fully established in France under Louis XIV — himself an accomplished dancer who performed in court ballets. The technical vocabulary of ballet remains French to this day.

From Old French boeuf (ox, beef). After the Norman Conquest of 1066, French became the language of the English ruling class. The English peasants who raised the animals kept their Germanic words (cow, pig, sheep, deer). The French-speaking nobles who ate the cooked meat used French words — beef, pork, mutton, venison. This split is why English has two words for the same animal.

From French cliché — the past participle of clicher, a printers' term for the sound made when a stereotype plate (a cast of a set of type) hit the molten metal. Printers kept common phrases in ready-made plates to save typesetting time. These frequently reused phrases gave us the figurative meaning: an overused expression.

From Anglo-French coeverfu, from Old French cuevre-feu — literally 'cover the fire.' Medieval towns required fires to be covered or extinguished at a fixed evening hour (signaled by a bell) to prevent house fires from spreading. The curfew bell told people to cover their hearths. The modern sense of restricting movement after dark evolved from this fire-safety rule.

From Old French desaise — literally 'not at ease,' discomfort. Originally meant simply a state of discomfort or unpleasantness, not necessarily illness. The medical narrowing to 'illness' happened gradually in the 14th–15th centuries as the word became specifically associated with bodily ailments. 'Ease' as comfort survives in 'at ease.'

From French grotesque, from Italian grottesca, 'of a grotto.' When Renaissance excavators uncovered ancient Roman rooms (called grotte, caves) in the late 15th century, they found elaborate decorative paintings with fantastical hybrid figures — humans merging with plants and animals. These bizarre cave paintings inspired a distinctive style called 'grotesque' decoration, later any bizarre or distorted form.

From Old French mortgage — literally 'dead pledge.' A mortgage was called a 'dead' pledge because either the property died to the creditor if the debtor defaulted, or the pledge died when the loan was repaid. 'Mort' (dead) is from Latin mori (to die); the same root gives us 'mortality,' 'mortuary,' and 'murder.'

From French naïf (feminine: naïve), meaning 'natural, native, unaffected.' The French word derived from Latin nativus (native, innate). Originally positive — describing natural, unspoiled simplicity — it gradually acquired the sense of 'lacking worldly experience' and then 'foolishly credulous.' The two dots over the i (diaeresis) show the two vowels are pronounced separately.

From Old French porc (pig). Like beef, the word shows the Norman class divide: English-speaking peasants raised pigs and kept the Germanic word (pig, swine), while French-speaking nobles who ate the cooked meat used the French term. 'Pork barrel' came much later, from American politics.

From French restaurant, 'something that restores,' from restaurer (to restore). The first restaurants appeared in Paris around 1765, offering restorative broths (bouillons restaurants) to the weak and sick. The idea of a place where individual diners chose from a menu and were served at tables was a revolutionary concept — before restaurants, you ate what was served at inns.

From French saboter (to walk noisily in wooden shoes, to work carelessly). Sabots were heavy wooden shoes worn by French workers. During labor disputes in the early 20th century, French workers reportedly threw their wooden shoes into machinery to halt production. Whether literally true or metaphorical, 'sabotage' entered the vocabulary of labor conflict — and later warfare.

Named after Étienne de Silhouette (1709–1767), a notoriously cheap French Controller-General of Finances. He imposed harsh austerity measures in 1759, cutting government spending so aggressively that his name became synonymous with cheapness and minimalism. Profile portraits cut from black paper — a simple, low-cost substitute for painted portraits — were mockingly called 'silhouettes' after him.

From Old French travail (labor, toil), from Vulgar Latin *tripaliare (to torture with a tripalium — a three-staked instrument of punishment). In medieval times, long journeys were genuinely dangerous, exhausting ordeals. 'Travelling' was hard work — identical in origin to 'travail.' The two words diverged in meaning as travel became safer over centuries, but started as the same word meaning agony.

From Old French vilain, meaning a peasant or serf — someone who worked on a villa (farm). Medieval aristocrats used the word as an insult for uncouth rural people, contrasting them with courtly nobility. Over centuries it drifted from 'low-class person' to 'wicked person,' reflecting class prejudice hardened into language.

root: Atlas (a Titan who held up the sky)
history: From the Greek Titan Atlas, who was condemned to hold up the celestial sphere for eternity. Flemish cartographer Gerardus Mercator used an image of Atlas holding the globe on the cover of his 1595 collection of maps. The image became standard, and the word 'atlas' came to mean any book of maps.

From Greek katastrophe, 'a sudden turn downward.' In ancient Greek drama, the catastrophe was the final act of a tragedy — the decisive turn of events leading to the ending. It carried the sense of an overturning or reversal of fortune, not just destruction.

From Greek khaos, meaning 'vast chasm or void' — the formless state before creation in Greek cosmology. In Hesiod's Theogony, Chaos was the first thing to exist, a yawning emptiness from which everything emerged. The modern sense of 'complete disorder' developed later.

From Greek demokratia — demos (the people) + kratos (rule, power). Coined in ancient Athens around the 5th century BC to describe a political system where citizens, not kings or nobles, held power. The Athenian version excluded women, slaves, and foreigners; the word's full meaning has expanded over 2,500 years of political thought.

From Greek gymnasion (place for athletic exercises), from gymnos (naked). Ancient Greek athletes competed and trained completely nude — practical for exercise and symbolizing equality in competition. The gymnasium was the place where you stripped down to train. Modern German kept the word for academic high schools; modern English uses it for sports halls.

From Marathon, a plain northeast of Athens where Greeks defeated a much larger Persian army in 490 BC. According to legend, the soldier Pheidippides ran 26 miles to Athens to announce the victory before collapsing and dying. The modern marathon race was created for the first modern Olympics in 1896, set at roughly that distance in honor of the legendary run.

From the Maeander (Maiandros) river in Phrygia (modern Turkey), famous in antiquity for its extreme winding course. The river twisted back on itself so dramatically that it became the classical example of a winding path. Greek mathematicians used its curves as examples in geometry; Roman artists used the meander pattern in decorative borders. The river's name became the word for any wandering path.

From Mentor, the trusted friend to whom Odysseus entrusted his household when he left for Troy. In the Odyssey, the goddess Athena takes the form of Mentor to guide Odysseus's son Telemachus. The name became an archetype for a wise, trusted guide — first popularized in this sense by François Fénelon's 1699 novel 'Les Aventures de Télémaque.'

From Greek mousike, 'art of the Muses.' The nine Muses were Greek goddesses who presided over arts and sciences. Mousike originally covered poetry, dance, and song together — all arts inspired by the Muses. The modern narrowing to organized sound came through Latin and Old French.

From Narcissus, the beautiful youth in Greek mythology who fell in love with his own reflection in a pool and wasted away staring at it, unable to leave. Some versions say he drowned trying to embrace the image. The narcissus flower grew where he died. Sigmund Freud adopted the myth in 1914 to describe excessive self-love in psychology.

From Greek panikon, 'of Pan.' The god Pan was a nature deity who supposedly caused sudden, irrational terror in travelers who disturbed his midday sleep in forests and mountains. Greeks attributed any sudden mass fright to Pan's anger. 'Panic attack' preserves this ancient belief.

From Greek paradeisos, borrowed from Old Iranian pairidaeza — a compound of pairi (around) + daeza (wall), meaning an enclosed garden or park. Persian kings maintained these walled pleasure gardens with exotic animals and plants. Greek writers used the word for the Garden of Eden.

From Greek philosophia — literally 'love of wisdom.' The term was reportedly coined by Pythagoras, who rejected the title 'wise man' (sophos) as too arrogant, preferring 'lover of wisdom.' The word reflects the Greek ideal that wisdom is pursued, never fully possessed.

Coined by British scientist John Herschel in 1839 from Greek phos/photos (light) + graphein (to write or draw). A photograph is literally 'writing with light.' Herschel also coined 'positive,' 'negative,' and 'snapshot' as photography terms. The Greek-root compound system lets scientists name inventions with descriptive precision.

From Greek skhole, meaning 'leisure' or 'discussion held during leisure time.' For ancient Greeks, learning was a leisure activity for free citizens — only people with time away from physical labor could pursue philosophy and debate. The idea that education is a luxury for the free-born is baked into the word itself.

Named after Tantalus, a king in Greek mythology who offended the gods and was punished in Hades by being made to stand in a pool of water beneath fruit trees. When he reached for the fruit, branches pulled away; when he bent to drink, the water receded. His name gave English the verb 'tantalize' — to tease with something desirable just out of reach.

Coined in the 19th century from Greek tele (far off) + phone (voice, sound). The elements were classical Greek but the compound word was new — invented to name a new technology for transmitting voice over distances. Alexander Graham Bell registered the patent in 1876. Many scientific coinages of the 19th–20th centuries follow this pattern of Greek-root compounds.

From Greek theatron (a place for seeing), from theasthai (to behold). Ancient Greek theaters were built into hillsides to create tiered seating with clear sightlines to the circular performance area below. The word preserves the primacy of the audience — a theater is defined by those who watch, not those who perform.

From Greek tragoidia — literally 'goat song.' Ancient Greek dramatic competitions (Dionysia) may have awarded a goat as a prize, or actors may have worn goatskins, or the chorus may have performed dressed as satyrs (goat-men). Scholars still debate the exact connection, but the word preserves this strange goat-theatrical origin of Western dramatic tradition.

From Greek theriake antidotos — antidote against wild animal venom. Medieval physicians sold a complex compound medicine called treacle, supposed to cure all poisons. The original formula was elaborate and expensive; cheaper versions eventually contained molasses as a base ingredient. By the 18th century, 'treacle' had come to mean the molasses itself — sweet medicine become pure sweetness.

From Greek typhon (whirlwind) — possibly reinforced by Chinese tai fung (great wind). The Greek Typhon was a monstrous storm-god with serpent legs who battled Zeus. English got the word through both Portuguese tufão (from Arabic tufan) and directly from Chinese, creating two parallel routes that converged on the same spelling.

From Latin harena or arena, meaning 'sand.' The floors of Roman amphitheaters and gladiatorial fighting grounds were covered with sand to absorb the blood of combatants and animals. The sand-covered fighting floor became synonymous with the performance space itself, then with any competitive venue. The amphitheater's sand pit became every sports arena.

From Latin calculus, meaning 'small stone.' The Romans used small stones (calculi) on counting boards for arithmetic — early abacus-style computation. Moving stones meant calculating. The same word gives us 'calculus' (the mathematical discipline Newton and Leibniz developed) and 'calcium' (the element named for limestone/chalk).

From Latin candidatus, 'dressed in white.' Roman citizens seeking public office wore a whitened toga (toga candida) to symbolize purity and distinction in crowds at the Forum. The white garment was a deliberate signal of someone seeking votes.

From Latin caput (head). A 'capital' city was the 'head' city of a region. Capital punishment was punishment involving the head (decapitation). In finance, capital was the 'head sum' or principal of an investment. The word also gives us 'cattle' (caput was used for livestock in accounting — 'per head'). All these meanings radiate from the Latin head.

From Italian carnevale, from medieval Latin carne vale — 'farewell to meat.' The Christian tradition of Lenten fasting (40 days without meat) was preceded by a period of feasting and celebration — a final indulgence before abstinence. The Venetian carnival became the most famous: days of masked revelers enjoying one last excess before the long fast. The masked festival became the word itself.

From Latin cerealis, 'of Ceres,' the Roman goddess of grain, agriculture, and fertility (equivalent to Greek Demeter). Grain crops were under her divine protection. The word was used in English in the early 19th century to describe grain plants; the breakfast food sense developed later, popularized by the American breakfast cereal industry of the 1890s.

Directly from Latin circus, meaning 'ring' or 'circle.' In ancient Rome, the Circus Maximus was a massive oval stadium for chariot races and public spectacles. The modern traveling circus with acrobats and animals adopted the name in the 18th century.

From Late Latin companio — literally 'one who eats bread with another.' In medieval military life, soldiers in the same unit shared food from a common supply. Someone you shared bread with was a companion. The word captures the bond formed by sharing a meal.

Possibly from Latin genu (knee), referring to the ancient Roman custom of a father acknowledging a newborn child as his own by placing it on his knee. A child so acknowledged was 'genuine' — truly the father's own. Alternative theories trace it to Latin genus (birth, race).

From Latin inaugurare — to take omens before beginning something important. Roman augurs were priests who interpreted divine will by observing the flight, feeding, and singing of birds. No major undertaking — a battle, a building, a new official's tenure — could begin without consulting the augurs. 'Inaugurate' preserves this sacred omen-checking at the start of something new.

From Latin manus (hand) + factura (a making), from facere (to make). Originally meant 'made by hand' — the opposite of what the word implies today. The Industrial Revolution transformed manufacturing from hand-craft to machine production, so the word now commonly refers to industrial-scale production. The hand is gone from the factory floor, but preserved in the name.

From Latin moneta, a title of Juno — the queen of the Roman gods. The Roman mint was located in the temple of Juno Moneta on the Capitoline Hill. Coins struck there were called moneta, then the coins themselves. Moneta became Old French moneie, then English 'money.' Juno's temple-mint gave the world its most essential economic concept.

From Latin musculus, meaning 'little mouse.' Romans thought the movement of muscles under the skin looked like a mouse moving under a blanket. The same Latin word also gives us mussel — the shellfish was thought to resemble a small mouse in its shell.

One of English's most dramatic meaning shifts. From Latin nescius (ignorant), Old French nice meant 'foolish, silly.' By the 14th century it had evolved to 'wanton, lascivious,' then 'coy, shy.' By the 15th century it meant 'refined, precise.' By the 18th century it had settled into 'agreeable, pleasant.' Five centuries of gradual improvement from 'stupid' to 'pleasant.'

From Latin Palatium, the Palatine Hill in Rome — one of the original seven hills of the city. Augustus Caesar built his home there, and subsequent emperors followed, making it the heart of imperial power. The hill's name became attached to grand imperial residences, then to any large royal or official building. The Palatine gave its name to palaces around the world.

From the Latin name of a Catholic Church committee founded in 1622: 'Congregatio de Propaganda Fide' (Congregation for Propagating the Faith). The committee organized missionary activity. In the 20th century, especially after World War I, the word took on its modern meaning of systematic state-sponsored information designed to influence public opinion.

From Latin rivalis, originally 'one who uses the same stream.' Neighboring farmers who drew water from the same river often competed — and fought — over access. The Latin root rivus (stream) captured the idea that shared resources breed competition.

From Latin salarium — the money paid to Roman soldiers to buy salt, once a precious and essential commodity. Salt was used to preserve food before refrigeration, making it as valuable as gold in some regions. The phrase 'worth his salt' comes from the same root.

Possibly from Latin sine (without) + cera (wax). According to one popular explanation, dishonest sculptors concealed flaws in marble statues with wax. Honest work was 'sine cera' — without wax. Etymologists debate this, with others tracing it to semel (once) + cera (create). Whichever origin is correct, the 'without wax' story is too good to abandon entirely.

From Latin trivium — the junction of three roads (tri- three + via road). In Roman cities, crossroads were common meeting points where people gathered to gossip and exchange news. Information shared at crossroads was considered 'trivial' — commonplace street-corner talk, not serious knowledge. The medieval university curriculum called the basic three subjects (grammar, logic, rhetoric) the trivium.

From Italian vulcano, from Latin Vulcanus — the Roman god of fire and metalworking (equivalent to Greek Hephaestus). The Romans believed Vulcan worked his divine forge beneath the Aeolian islands north of Sicily, and that volcanic eruptions were the smoke and sparks from his smithy. The island of Vulcano in Italy gave its name to all fire-spewing mountains.

Originally meant 'inspiring awe and reverence' — full of awe. An awful God was one deserving profound reverence and fear. Through the 19th century, 'awful' meant either 'awe-inspiring' or 'terrible,' both senses active simultaneously. By the 20th century, only the negative sense survived. The original positive meaning lives on in 'awesome.'

A corruption of 'Bethlem' — short for the Hospital of Saint Mary of Bethlehem in London, founded in 1247. By the 14th century it housed the mentally ill, and by the 16th century it was notorious for the chaos and screaming within its walls. Visitors paid to watch the inmates as a form of entertainment. 'Bedlam' became a byword for chaotic disorder.

Originally a World War II term for aerial bombs large enough to destroy an entire city block. The Royal Air Force used 'blockbusters' in bombing raids over Germany. After the war, real estate agents used the term for houses sold to Black families in white neighborhoods to 'bust' the racial block. By the 1970s, it had shifted entirely to entertainment: a film or book so successful it dominates the market.

From Middle English banefire — literally 'bone fire.' Large fires fueled by bones (of plague victims, criminals, and livestock) were a common medieval practice for both sanitation and celebration. The Midsummer fires in Scotland were specifically bone fires. The word softened over centuries as the charnel association faded, leaving only the festive meaning.

From Old English cleowen (ball of yarn or thread). In Greek mythology, Ariadne gave Theseus a ball of thread to unspool as he entered the Minotaur's labyrinth, letting him find his way back out. The clew — the thread — was what led to the solution. By the 16th century, 'clue' (a spelling variant of clew) had acquired the figurative meaning of any piece of evidence that guides toward an answer.

From Old English niht (night) + mære, an evil spirit in Germanic and Norse folklore that was believed to sit on sleepers' chests causing feelings of suffocation and terror. The 'mare' was a goblin or demon, not a female horse. 'Night-mare' literally meant 'night-goblin.' The horse meaning is a complete coincidence of spelling.

Old English sælig meant 'blessed, happy, fortunate' — a very positive word. By Middle English it had shifted to 'pious, innocent.' Then 'innocent' drifted to 'naive, simple.' By the 16th century it meant 'foolish.' The word's journey from 'divinely blessed' to 'foolish' took about 500 years, one of English's most dramatic semantic reversals.

From Old Norse angr, meaning 'grief' or 'sorrow' — a wider emotional suffering rather than specifically hot temper. Related to Old Norse angra (to distress). The shift from general anguish to specifically hostile emotion happened in English after the Norse settlement. The root also connects to 'anguish.'

Old Norse berserkr described Norse warriors who fought in a frenzied, trance-like state of extreme violence. The name likely means 'bear-shirt' — either referring to warriors who wore bear skins into battle, or to bear-like ferocity. English borrowed the word from Walter Scott's 1822 novel 'The Pirate.'

Old English had the word ey for egg, but Norse settlers in northern England used egg. For centuries, the two words coexisted in different parts of England — a source of genuine confusion. William Caxton complained in 1490 that a merchant in Kent could not buy eggs because the local word was still ey. Norse egg eventually won everywhere.

From Old Norse ský, meaning 'cloud.' Old English had a perfectly good word for the heavens — heofon (heaven) — but Norse settlers who colonized northern England in the 9th century brought ský with them. The word shifted meaning from 'cloud' to the entire vault above us, replacing heofon in everyday speech.

From Old Norse uggligr, 'to be dreaded or feared,' from ugga (to fear). The original meaning was 'frightening or menacing' — something that inspired dread. The shift from 'frightening' to 'unattractive in appearance' happened gradually in Middle English. Something ugly was originally dangerous, not just displeasing.

From Old Norse vindauga — literally 'wind-eye,' a compound of vindr (wind) + auga (eye). Norse settlers in Britain introduced this word, replacing the Old English eagþyrl (eye-hole). Early windows were simply openings in walls to let in air and light — holes for the wind to look through.

From Hindi bangla, meaning 'of or belonging to Bengal.' The style of low, wide-roofed thatched house common in Bengal was adopted by British colonial officers seeking relief from the tropical heat. The term was brought back to Britain and eventually spread worldwide to describe any single-story house with a wide porch.

From Nahuatl xocolatl — a compound of xococ (bitter) + atl (water). The Aztecs drank chocolate cold, mixed with chili peppers and ground corn, as a bitter ritual beverage. Spanish conquistadors brought it to Europe in the 16th century. Europeans added sugar and heated it, transforming a bitter ceremonial drink into a sweet luxury.

From Italian disastro — literally 'bad star.' Medieval astrologers believed that the positions of stars and planets directly caused catastrophic events on Earth. An unfavorable celestial alignment was a 'bad star' event — a disaster. The same belief gives us 'ill-starred.'

Named after John Duns Scotus, a brilliant medieval philosopher whose 'Dunsmen' followers dominated European universities. Renaissance humanists mocked the Dunsmen as hopelessly rigid and backward-looking, making 'Duns' an insult for anyone resistant to new learning. A follower of the genius Duns Scotus became synonymous with stupidity — one of history's crueler ironies.

A Scottish variant of 'grammar.' In medieval times, literacy and learning were associated with magic — only learned scholars and clergy could read, and their knowledge seemed supernatural to the illiterate majority. 'Grammar' came to mean occult knowledge in Scottish dialects, and 'glamour' was the enchantment cast by such magic. It later shifted to mean worldly enchantment and allure.

From Spanish huracán, from Taino huracán — the storm god in the indigenous Taino religion of the Caribbean. When Spanish explorers first encountered these massive Atlantic storms in the 15th century, they adopted the indigenous name. The Taino people, nearly exterminated by colonization, left their language in English through words like hurricane, hammock, canoe, and barbecue.

From Hindi jangal, from Sanskrit jangala (rough, arid terrain). In Hindi, jangal referred to any uncultivated land, not specifically dense tropical forest. British colonial officers in India adopted the word for dense tropical growth. The lush, dangerous jungle of popular imagination came from British accounts of India, not from the original meaning of sparse wasteland.

From Hokkien Chinese kê-tsiap or Malay kicap, a salty sauce made from fermented fish or shellfish. British sailors encountered it in Southeast Asia and brought the concept home. Early British ketchups were made from mushrooms, walnuts, or anchovies — no tomatoes. American cooks added tomatoes in the early 19th century, creating what we now recognize as ketchup.

From Italian quarantina giorni, 'forty days.' During the Black Death in the 14th century, ships arriving in Venice were required to anchor offshore for 40 days (quaranta giorni) before passengers could disembark — a period believed sufficient to reveal any plague. The 40-day period echoed biblical periods of trial (40 days of flood, 40 years of wandering). Shortened to quarantine, the practice spread across Europe.

Origin genuinely unknown — one of English's great etymological mysteries. A popular but unverified legend says a Dublin theater owner in 1791 bet he could introduce a new word to the language overnight, chalked 'quiz' on walls across the city, and the next morning everyone was asking what it meant. More likely it derives from Latin quis (who) or is an altered form of 'inquisition.'

From Czech robota (forced labor, drudgery), from rab (slave). The word was coined by writer Karel Čapek in his 1920 play 'R.U.R.' (Rossum's Universal Robots), about artificial beings manufactured to do human labor. It is one of very few words with a precisely documented birthday. Čapek credited his brother Josef with suggesting the word.

Named after John Montagu (1718–1792), 4th Earl of Sandwich, who according to legend asked for meat between two slices of bread so he could eat without leaving the card table. Whether the story is literally true, the Earl was certainly famous for his gambling. His title name for the snack spread from English high society into all European languages.

Coined by Horace Walpole in 1754 in a letter, from the Persian fairy tale 'The Three Princes of Serendip' (Serendip being an old Arabic/Persian name for Sri Lanka). In the tale, three princes make discoveries by accident and sagacity. Walpole invented 'serendipity' to describe the faculty of making happy accidental discoveries. It is one of the few words with a precisely documented coinage date and inventor.

From Hindi chāmpo, the imperative of chāmpnā (to press, knead). In India, head massage — including scalp pressing — was a common service at bathhouses. British colonists in 18th-century India encountered this practice and brought the word home. The original meaning was the massage itself; the hair-washing product came later.

From Scottish Gaelic sluagh-ghairm, the battle cry of a Scottish Highland clan — a compound of sluagh (army, host) + gairm (shout, cry). Highland clans shouted their family war cry when charging into battle. English writers first encountered these dramatic battle cries during Scottish conflicts. By the 19th century 'slogan' had transferred to any striking political or advertising phrase.

From Japanese taikun (great lord), composed of tai (great) + kun (lord, ruler). American sailors and diplomats who visited Japan in the 1850s brought the title back to describe the Japanese shogun. President Lincoln's cabinet used 'Tycoon' as a nickname for Lincoln himself. By the late 19th century it had transferred to powerful business magnates.

From Scottish Gaelic uisce beatha (Irish: uisce beathadh) — literally 'water of life.' Medieval monks distilled strong spirits and called them aqua vitae (Latin: water of life). When Gaelic speakers adopted distilling, they translated the Latin phrase into their own language. Uisce beatha gradually contracted to 'whiskey' in English mouths. The French did the same with water-of-life brandies, calling them eau-de-vie.

From Sanskrit yoga, meaning 'union' or 'discipline,' from the root yuj (to yoke or harness). The spiritual and physical practices of yoga aim to yoke or unite the individual self with universal consciousness. The word is related to the English word 'yoke' through a shared Proto-Indo-European ancestor.
